en.extract:
The zebra finches are two species of estrildid finch in the genus Taeniopygia found in Australia and Indonesia. They are seed-eaters that travel in large flocks.


Taxonomy
The genus Taeniopygia was introduced in 1862 by the German naturalist Ludwig Reichenbach. He included two species in the genus but did not specify the type. The type was designated in 1890 as Amadina castanotis Gould, the Australian zebra finch, by Richard Bowdler Sharpe. The genus name is from Ancient Greek ταινια/tainia meaning "band" and -πυγιος/-pugios meaning "-rumped".


Species
The genus contains two species:

Previously, both species were classified as a single species, the zebra finch (T. guttata). However, they were split by the IUCN Red List and BirdLife International in 2016. The International Ornithological Congress followed suit in 2022 based on studies noting differences in plumage, mtDNA divergence, and assortative mating between both species in captivity.
The zebra finch was first captured in 1801 during Nicolas Baudin's expedition to Australia. The Indonesian species was described in 1817 by Louis Pierre Vieillot in his Nouveau Dictionnaire d'Histoire Naturelle, where he gave it the scientific name Fringilla guttata. The Australian species was then described in 1837 by John Gould as Amadina castanotis. Its current genus, Taeniopygia, was described in 1862 by Ludwig Reichenbach. It is placed in the tribe Poephilini, along with the genus Poephila, which it was previously included in; the split between Taeniopygia and Poephila is justified by a 1987 study using protein electrophoresis and chromosomal banding.
The zebra finches likely evolved in Australia, with either northern or southeastern Australia postulated as two places where the genus arose. The present-day distribution of the species T. guttata is likely due to a Pleistocene glaciation event where the sea level dropped between about 100 and 150 metres (330 and 490 ft), putting the coasts of Timor and Australia closer. This allowed T. castanotis swept out to sea by cyclones to see mountains near the west coast of Timor, which prompted them to make landfall on the island.
The morphological differences between the species include differences in size. T. guttata is smaller than T. castanotis. In addition, the T. guttata males do not have the fine barring found on the throat and upper breast and have smaller breast bands. Incubation lasts for 14 days.
Although the Sunda zebra finch was described first, the Australian zebra finch is the far more famous member of the genus, due to its status as a popular pet as well as a model organism for the wider study of birds. The Australian zebra finch is used worldwide in several research fields (e.g. neurobiology, physiology, behaviour, ecology and evolution) as individuals are easy to maintain and breed in captivity.
Zebra finches are more social than many migratory birds, generally traveling in small bands and sometimes gathering in larger groups. They are one of the bird species that is able to learn new vocalizations and have become a dominant model species in the study of vocal learning. There is evidence that some aspects of this are culturally transmitted and that the songs of geographically distant populations can change over time, resulting in new dialects. Research also shows that zebra finches hear and respond to variations in bird song that are not apparent to human listeners. Female zebra finches show a preference for mates with a dialect similar to the one of their adolescent peers. Researchers are exploring analogies between human language and birdsong.


References

''[[Australian zebra finch|T. castanotis]]'' }} The '''zebra finches''' are two [[species]] of [[Estrildidae|estrildid finch]] in the genus '''''Taeniopygia''''' found in [[Australia]] and [[Indonesia]]. They are seed-eaters that travel in large flocks. ==Taxonomy== The genus ''Taeniopygia'' was introduced in 1862 by the German naturalist [[Ludwig Reichenbach]]. He included two species in the genus but did not specify the [[type species|type]].{{ cite book | last=Reichenbach | first=Ludwig | author-link=Ludwig Reichenbach | year=1862 | title=Die Singvögel als Fortsetzung de vollständigsten Naturgeschichte und zugleich als Central-Atlas für zoologische Gärten und für Thierfreunde. Jr | year=1968 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=14 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=357 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14481558 }} ==Species== The genus contains two species:{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. However, they were split by the [[IUCN Red List]] and [[BirdLife International]] in 2016. The [[International Ornithologists' Union|International Ornithological Congress]] followed suit in 2022 based on studies noting differences in plumage, [[Mitochondrial DNA|mtDNA]] divergence, and [[assortative mating]] between both species in captivity.{{sfn|Olsson|Alström|2020|p=106757}} The zebra finch was first captured in 1801 during [[Nicolas Baudin]]'s [[Baudin expedition to Australia|expedition]] to Australia. The Indonesian species was [[Scientific description|described]] in 1817 by [[Louis Pierre Vieillot]] in his ''Nouveau Dictionnaire d'Histoire Naturelle'', where he gave it the scientific name ''Fringilla guttata''. The Australian species was then described in 1837 by [[John Gould]] as ''Amadina castanotis''.{{sfn|Zann|1996|pp=xiii–xiv}} Its current genus, ''Taeniopygia'', was described in 1862 by [[Ludwig Reichenbach]]. It is placed in the tribe Poephilini,{{sfn|Zann|1996|p=5}} along with the genus ''[[Poephila]]'', which it was previously included in;{{sfn|Christidis|1987a|pp=380–392}} the split between ''Taeniopygia'' and ''Poephila'' is justified by a 1987 study using [[protein electrophoresis]] and [[Chromosome banding|chromosomal banding]].{{sfn|Christidis|1987b|pp=119–123}} The zebra finches likely evolved in Australia, with either northern or southeastern Australia postulated as two places where the genus arose. The present-day distribution of the species ''T. guttata'' is likely due to a Pleistocene glaciation event where the sea level dropped between about {{convert|100|and|150|m|ft}}, putting the coasts of Timor and Australia closer. This allowed ''T. castanotis'' swept out to sea by cyclones to see mountains near the west coast of Timor, which prompted them to make landfall on the island.{{sfn|Zann|1996|pp=9–10}} The [[Morphology (biology)|morphological]] differences between the species include differences in size. ''T. guttata'' is smaller than ''T. castanotis''. In addition, the ''T. guttata'' males do not have the fine barring found on the throat and upper breast and have smaller breast bands.{{sfn|Payne|2018}} Incubation lasts for 14 days.{{Cite journal |last1=Murray |first1=Jessica R. |last2=Varian-Ramos |first2=Claire W. |last3=Welch |first3=Zoe S. |last4=Saha |first4=Margaret S. |date=2013 |title=Embryological staging of the Zebra Finch, Taeniopygia Guttata |journal=Journal of Morphology |volume=274 |issue=10 |pages=1090–1110 |doi=10.1002/jmor.20165|pmid=23813920 |pmc=4239009 }} Although the Sunda zebra finch was described first, the Australian zebra finch is the far more famous member of the genus, due to its status as a popular pet as well as a [[model organism]] for the wider study of birds.{{sfn|Mello|2014|pp=1237–1242}} The Australian zebra finch is used worldwide in several research fields (e.g. neurobiology, physiology, behaviour, ecology and evolution) as individuals are easy to maintain and breed in captivity.{{Cite journal |last1=Griffith |first1=Simon C. |last2=Buchanan |first2=Katherine L. |date=September 2010 |title=The Zebra Finch: the ultimate Australian supermodel |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1071/MUv110n3_ED |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |language=en |volume=110 |issue=3 |pages=v–xii |doi=10.1071/MUv110n3_ED |bibcode=2010EmuAO.110D...5G |issn=0158-4197|url-access=subscription }} Zebra finches are more social than many migratory birds, generally traveling in small bands and sometimes gathering in larger groups.{{sfn|Mason|2022}} They are one of the bird species that is able to learn new [[bird vocalization|vocalizations]] and have become a dominant model species in the study of [[vocal learning]].{{sfn|Hyland Bruno|Jarvis|Liberman|Tchernichovski|2021|pp=449–472}} There is evidence that some aspects of this are culturally transmitted and that the songs of geographically distant populations can change over time, resulting in new dialects. Research also shows that zebra finches hear and respond to variations in bird song that are not apparent to human listeners. Female zebra finches show a preference for mates with a dialect similar to the one of their adolescent peers.{{sfn|Wang|Forstmeier|Farine|Maldonado-Chaparro|2022}} Researchers are exploring analogies between human language and birdsong.{{sfn|Mason|2022}}{{sfn|Hyland Bruno|Jarvis|Liberman|Tchernichovski|2021|pp=449–472}} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Cited sources == {{Refbegin}} *{{cite journal | title = Biochemical systematics within palaeotropic finches (Aves: Estrildidae) | last = Christidis | first = L. | journal = The Auk | year = 1987a | volume = 104 | issue = 3 | pages = 380–392 | doi = 10.2307/4087534 | issn = 0004-8038 | jstor = 4087534 | doi-access = free }} *{{cite journal | title = Phylogeny and Systematics of Estrildine Finches and Their Relationships to Other Seed-eating Passerines | last = Christidis | first = L. | journal = Emu – Austral Ornithology | year = 1987b | volume = 87 | issue = 2 | pages = 119–123 | doi = 10.1071/MU9870119 | bibcode = 1987EmuAO..87..119C | issn = 0158-4197 }} *{{cite journal | title = Birdsong Learning and Culture: Analogies with Human Spoken Language | last1 = Hyland Bruno | first1 = Julia | last2 = Jarvis | first2 = Erich D. | last3 = Liberman | first3 = Mark | last4 = Tchernichovski | first4 = Ofer | journal = Annual Review of Linguistics | date = 14 January 2021 | volume = 7 | issue = 1 | pages = 449–472 | doi = 10.1146/annurev-linguistics-090420-121034 | s2cid = 228894898 | issn = 2333-9683 }} *{{cite magazine| title = Do birds have language?
